request.question: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
request.answer: liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

title: 128th Infantry Regiment (United States)
José María Lafragua
The Epic of American Civilization
History of Oaxaca
The Old Gringo
Aureliano Blanquet
Mexican Youth Athenaeum
Benito Juárez
Agnes Salm-Salm
George Puflea
sentences: The 128th Infantry Regiment ("Les Terribles") is a United States military unit of the Wisconsin National Guard.
The 128th has served as part of the American Civil War, Spanish–American War, Mexican Civil War, World War I, World War II and the Iraq War.
José María Ibarra Lafragua was a Mexican liberal lawyer, politician, diplomat, and writer.
He was born in Puebla, 2 April 1813, the son of Lt. Col. José María Lafragua and Mariana de Ibarra and Veytia.
He served in as Minister of Interior in the government of liberal President Ignacio Comonfort.
He was the first director of the National Library of Mexico and key author of the Mexican Civil and Penal Codes.
The Epic of American Civilization is a mural by the social realist painter José Clemente Orozco.
It is located in the basement reading room of the Baker Memorial Library on the campus of Dartmouth College in Hanover, New Hampshire.

The mural, painted between 1932 and 1934, consists of a series of 24 fresco panels, whose principal themes are the impact of both indigenous Native Americans and European colonists on North America, and the impact of war (particularly the Mexican Civil War and the First World War) and rapid industrialization on the human spirit.
The mural was designated a National Historic Landmark in 2013.
In the Central Valley region of the Southern Mexican state of Oaxaca archeologists discovered evidence of historic settlements.

Aztecs from Tenochtitlan on the volcanic plateau to the North around what today is Mexico City first arrived in this region around 1250 AD establishing military rule in the 15th century until the arrival of the Spanish.
After the fall of Tenochtitlan, the Spanish took over Oaxaca which led to the eventual decrease of the Native population and the increase in African slaves.
The region was then settled by mostly Spanish immigrants from Europe and the African slaves they brought with them.
Oaxaca was considered a department after the Mexican War of Independence, but after the fall of emperor Agustín de Iturbide, it became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor.
During the 19th century, Oaxaca was split between liberal and conservative factions.
The political and military struggles between the factions resulted in wars and intrigues.
A series of major disasters occurred in the state from the 1920s to the 1940s.
In the 1940s and 1950s, new infrastructure projects were begun.
From the 1980s to the present, there has been much development of the tourism industry in the state.
The Old Gringo (Spanish: "Gringo Viejo" ) is a novel by Carlos Fuentes, written from 1964 to 1984 and first published in 1985.
Fuentes stated: "What started this novel was my admiration for Ambrose Bierce and for his "Tales of Soldiers and Civilians".
I was fascinated with the idea of a man who fought in the United States Civil War and dies in a Mexican civil war."
The novel addresses themes of death, cultural exchange, and Mexican identity, among others.
Its English-language translation became the first novel by a Mexican author to become a U.S. bestseller.
The book was one of three nominees for the Ritz Paris Hemingway Award as best novel of 1985.
Aureliano Blanquet (Morelia, Michoacán, 31 December 1849 – 15 April 1919 Barranca de Chavaxtla, Huatusco, Veracruz) was a general of the Federal Army during the Mexican Civil War.
He was a key participant in the coup d'état during the Decena trágica.
Blanquet has been identified "as one of the major villains of the Mexican Revolution".

The Mexican Youth Athenaeum, later known as the "Athenaeum of Mexico", was a Mexican civil association founded on October 28, 1909 with the purpose of working in favor of culture and art, by means of organization public meetings and debates.

Born as a response of a generation of young intellectuals who in the decline of the rule of President Porfirio Díaz set a series of criticisms to determinism and mechanism set by the Comtian and Spencerian positivism as the development model of Porfirio Díaz's administration and the group of the "científicos. Through a series of conferences and different cultural efforts they activated a reflexive awareness on education.
Benito Pablo Juárez García (] ; 21 March 1806 – 18 July 1872) was a Mexican lawyer and liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

Agnes Salm-Salm (December 25, 1844 – December 21, 1912) was the American wife of Prince Felix zu Salm-Salm, a Prussian mercenary beside whom she played a role in the American Civil War, the Mexican Civil War between President Benito Juárez and the Austrian archduke Maximilian I of Mexico, and the Franco-Prussian War.
George Puflea Craciun was an early aviator.
Born in modern-day Romania, he flew in the First Balkan War, in the Mexican Revolution, in the First World War and in early American and Mexican civil aviation.
He was also known under his name in Spanish, .

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.question: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
response.gold_answer: liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

response.hop1_query: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
hop1_docs: History of Oaxaca | In the Central Valley region of the Southern Mexican state of Oaxaca archeologists discovered evidence of historic settlements.  Aztecs from Tenochtitlan on the volcanic plateau to the North around what today is Mexico City first arrived in this region around 1250 AD establishing military rule in the 15th century until the arrival of the Spanish.  After the fall of Tenochtitlan, the Spanish took over Oaxaca which led to the eventual decrease of the Native population and the increase in African slaves.  The region was then settled by mostly Spanish immigrants from Europe and the African slaves they brought with them.  Oaxaca was considered a department after the Mexican War of Independence, but after the fall of emperor Agustín de Iturbide, it became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor.  During the 19th century, Oaxaca was split between liberal and conservative factions.  The political and military struggles between the factions resulted in wars and intrigues.  A series of major disasters occurred in the state from the 1920s to the 1940s.  In the 1940s and 1950s, new infrastructure projects were begun.  From the 1980s to the present, there has been much development of the tourism industry in the state.

Capture of Oaxaca (1812) | The Capture of Oaxaca was a battle during the War of Mexican Independence that occurred on 25 November 1812 at Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  It was fought between the royalist forces loyal to the Spanish crown, commanded by General Antonio Pío González-Saravia Mollinedo, and the Mexican rebels fighting for independence from the Spanish Empire, commanded by José María Morelos y Pavón.  The battle resulted in a victory for the Mexican rebels.

Félix Díaz (politician) | Félix Díaz Velasco (17 February 18689 July 1945) was a Mexican politician and general born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  He graduated as an engineer from the Colegio Militar in 1888.  He was a leading figure in the rebellion against President Francisco I. Madero during the Mexican Revolution.

Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca | The Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca (Spanish: "Marquesado del Valle de Oaxaca" ) is a hereditary marquisal title in the Spanish nobility and a former seignorial estate in New Spain.  It was granted to Don Hernán Cortés, "conquistador" who led the conquest of the Aztec Empire, by Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor in 1529.  Despite its name, the marquisate covered a much larger area than the Valley of Oaxaca, comprising a vast stretch of land in the present-day Mexican states of Oaxaca, Morelos, Veracruz, Michoacán and Mexico.

Copa de Zaachila | The Copa de Zaachila is presented each year by the President of the Mexican Baseball League to the team that wins the Serie del Rey.  Recent trophy designs contain the design of a china cup found in the archeological zone of Zaachila, Oaxaca.

Libyan Civil War (2011–present) | The Libyan Civil War refers to the ongoing conflicts in Libya, beginning with the Arab Spring protests of 2011, which led to the First Libyan Civil War, foreign military intervention, and the ousting and death of Muammar Gaddafi.  The civil war's aftermath and proliferation of armed groups led to violence and instability across the country, which erupted into renewed civil war in 2014.  The ongoing crisis in Libya has so far resulted in tens of thousands of casualties since the onset of violence in early 2011.  During both civil wars, the output of Libya's economically crucial oil industry collapsed to a small fraction of its usual level, with most facilities blockaded or damaged by rival groups, despite having the largest oil reserves of any African country.  U.S. President Barack Obama stated on 11 April 2016 that not preparing for a post-Gaddafi Libya was probably the "worst mistake" of his presidency.

Monte Albán | Monte Albán, likely originally called Danipaguache (Zapotec for sacred mountain of life), is a large pre-Columbian archaeological site in the Santa Cruz Xoxocotlán Municipality in the southern Mexican state of Oaxaca (17.043° N, 96.767°W).  The site is located on a low mountainous range rising above the plain in the central section of the Valley of Oaxaca where the latter's northern Etla, eastern Tlacolula, and southern Zimatlán & Ocotlán (or Valle Grande) branches meet.  The present-day state capital Oaxaca City is located approximately 9 km east of Monte Albán.
hop1_titles: Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Oaxaca history and individuals connected to Oaxaca.

- "became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor." (passage 1)
- "The Capture of Oaxaca ... occurred on 25 November 1812" and commanders: "General Antonio Pío González-Saravia Mollinedo" and "José María Morelos y Pavón." (passage 2)
- "Félix Díaz Velasco ... was a Mexican politician and general born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca." and he was "a leading figure in the rebellion against President Francisco I. Madero during the Mexican Revolution." (passage 3)
- Passage 3 contains a garbled date string for Félix Díaz: "(17 February 18689 July 1945)" — date information ambiguous. (passage 3)
- No passage names a president described as "present for the Mexican Civil War" or explicitly links a named president from Oaxaca to a conflict called "Mexican Civil War." (all passages)

Ambiguities / missing facts:
- "Mexican Civil War" is ambiguous (could mean Reform War / War of the Reform, Mexican Revolution, etc.) — unresolved.
- Missing fact: explicit name of a "president ... from Oaxaca" who was "present for the Mexican Civil War." Bridge entity likely needed: a president from Oaxaca (candidate not present in passages).

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Benito Juárez" — inspect lede/infobox, "Early life", "Presidency", and sections on the Reform War/War of the Reform.
2. "War of the Reform" (a.k.a. Reform War, Guerra de Reforma) — lede and timeline to confirm whether it is the conflict referred to as "Mexican Civil War" and which presidents participated.
3. "Mexican Civil War (disambiguation)" or search keywords: "Mexican Civil War meaning", "Mexican Civil War Reform War Revolution" to disambiguate the term.
4. "List of presidents of Mexico" or search "presidents of Mexico from Oaxaca" (sections/infoboxes) to find any president born in Oaxaca and their involvement in named conflicts.
response.hop2_query: Benito Juárez Oaxaca Reform War president
hop2_docs: Benito Juárez Autonomous University of Oaxaca | The Benito Juárez Autonomous University of Oaxaca (Spanish: Universidad Autónoma Benito Juárez de Oaxaca , UABJO) is a public university located in the city of Oaxaca de Juárez in state of Oaxaca, Mexico.
Benito Juárez | Benito Pablo Juárez García (] ; 21 March 1806 – 18 July 1872) was a Mexican lawyer and liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

Benito Juárez National Park | The Benito Juárez National Park is in the Valles Centrales Region of Oaxaca, Mexico, and includes parts of the municipalities of San Felipe Tejalapam and San Andres Huayapan.
Estadio Benito Juárez | Estadio Benito Juárez is a stadium in Oaxaca, Mexico.  It holds about 10,250 people and is primarily used for soccer.  The construction began in 1984, the opening was in 1987.

Benito Juárez (Orozco) | Benito Juárez is an outdoor bronze sculpture of Benito Juárez by Moises Cabrera Orozco, located in Bryant Park in Manhattan, New York.  Donated by the State of Oaxaca on behalf of the Mexican Government and the Mexican Trade Center, the portrait sculpture was cast in Mexico in 2002 and installed on October 9, 2004.  It is the most recent statue in the park, and the first to depict a Mexican.
XEUBJ-AM | XEUBJ-AM is a Mexican university radio station owned by the Universidad Autónoma Benito Juárez de Oaxaca in Oaxaca, Oaxaca.

Félix María Zuloaga | Félix María Zuloaga Trillo (31 March 1813 – 11 February 1898) was a Mexican general and a Conservative leader in the War of Reform.  In the late 1850s and early 1860s, Zuloaga served as unconstitutional interim conservative president of Mexico in opposition to the constitutional president Benito Juárez of the Liberal Party.
hop2_titles: Benito Juárez Autonomous University of Oaxaca
response.summary_2: Benito Pablo Juárez García was the constitutional president during the War of Reform. Benito Pablo Juárez García was a Mexican lawyer and liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.
